title: Azure CI/CD workflow
summary: Azure DevOps connects source code, build steps, tests, approvals, secrets, and deployment targets into one automated release workflow.
content:
## How Azure works in CI/CD

Azure DevOps helps automate CI/CD pipelines by connecting source code, build processes, testing, and deployments into one workflow. When code is pushed, Azure Pipelines can automatically build, test, and deploy the application.

## Typical frontend flow

For a React or Next.js app, the pipeline checks out the repository, installs dependencies, runs linting or tests, creates a production build, publishes build artifacts, and deploys to staging or production.

## Simple Azure CI/CD flow

- Git push to GitHub or Azure Repos
- Azure Pipeline trigger starts
- Install dependencies
- Run tests, linting, and build
- Publish artifacts
- Deploy to Azure App Service, Azure Storage, Kubernetes, or another target
- Use environment approvals before production

## Enterprise safety

Azure supports environment approvals, rollback handling, secret management through Azure Key Vault, build logs, release history, and monitoring hooks. This makes releases consistent and easier to audit.

## Interview line

Azure CI/CD reduces manual deployment effort and ensures consistent releases across environments.

code_examples.title: Simple Azure Pipeline for a React app
code_examples.code:
trigger:
- main

steps:
- script: npm install
  displayName: Install dependencies
- script: npm run build
  displayName: Build application

related_questions.preview: How does Azure work in CI/CD?
What is your CI/CD setup and what checks do you run in the pipeline?

title: Azure DevOps
summary: Azure DevOps is Microsoft's suite for planning work, storing code, running pipelines, and managing delivery.
content:
## What it includes

Azure DevOps is not just one tool. It includes Azure Boards for work tracking, Azure Repos for Git repositories, Azure Pipelines for CI/CD, Azure Artifacts for packages, and Test Plans for QA workflows.

## In deployment work

In frontend projects, teams usually use Azure DevOps to connect the repository to a pipeline. A code push or pull request can trigger linting, type checking, tests, builds, artifact publishing, and deployment.

## Why companies use it

Enterprise teams like Azure DevOps because permissions, audit history, approvals, service connections, secrets, and environment controls are built into one ecosystem.

related_questions.preview: How does Azure work in CI/CD?

title: Azure Pipelines
summary: Azure Pipelines is the CI/CD engine that runs jobs on agents whenever a trigger, schedule, or manual release starts.
content:
## What a pipeline does

Azure Pipelines executes the steps required to verify and release code. For a frontend app, that usually means install, lint, test, build, publish artifacts, and deploy.

## Agents and jobs

A pipeline runs on an agent. Microsoft-hosted agents are managed by Azure, while self-hosted agents run inside a company's own network when deployments need private access.

## Triggers

Common triggers are pushes to `main`, pull requests, scheduled nightly builds, and manual production releases.

code_examples.title: Pipeline trigger sketch
code_examples.code:
trigger:
- main

pool:
  vmImage: ubuntu-latest

steps:
- script: npm ci
- script: npm test
- script: npm run build

related_questions.preview: How does Azure work in CI/CD?

title: YAML pipeline files
summary: YAML pipeline files define CI/CD as code so build, test, and deploy steps live beside the application source.
content:
## Why YAML is used

YAML makes the pipeline version-controlled. If the app changes, the build and deployment instructions can change in the same pull request.

## Common pieces

A typical YAML pipeline defines triggers, variables, stages, jobs, steps, scripts, artifacts, environments, and approvals.

## Interview wording

I usually describe YAML pipelines as deployment instructions stored as code. Instead of clicking through a UI every time, the team reviews pipeline changes through pull requests.

code_examples.title: Stage-based pipeline sketch
code_examples.code:
stages:
- stage: Build
  jobs:
  - job: Web
    steps:
    - script: npm ci
    - script: npm run build

- stage: Deploy
  dependsOn: Build
  jobs:
  - job: DeployWeb
    steps:
    - script: echo Deploy artifact

related_questions.preview: How does Azure work in CI/CD?

title: Azure App Service deployments
summary: Azure App Service hosts web applications and is a common target for deploying React, Next.js, Node.js, and API apps.
content:
## Where it fits

After the pipeline builds the app, Azure App Service can receive the generated files or Node.js application package.

## Staging slots

A common enterprise pattern is deploying to a staging slot first, testing the release, and then swapping the slot into production. This reduces downtime and gives teams a safer rollback path.

## Frontend example

A React build folder can be deployed as static assets. A Next.js app may need a Node runtime or a container depending on how it is built and hosted.

related_questions.preview: How does Azure work in CI/CD?

title: Azure Container Registry
summary: Azure Container Registry stores Docker images that pipelines build and later deploy to container-based environments.
content:
## What ACR stores

Azure Container Registry stores versioned container images. A pipeline can build a Docker image, tag it with a commit SHA or version number, push it to ACR, and deploy that exact image.

## Why it helps

The same image can move through staging and production, which reduces environment drift. If production has a problem, the team can roll back to a previous image tag.

## Common targets

ACR is often paired with Azure Kubernetes Service, Azure Container Apps, or App Service for Containers.

code_examples.title: Container build sketch
code_examples.code:
docker build -t myapp:$(Build.BuildId) .
docker tag myapp:$(Build.BuildId) myregistry.azurecr.io/myapp:$(Build.BuildId)
docker push myregistry.azurecr.io/myapp:$(Build.BuildId)

related_questions.preview: How do you use Docker in a frontend development context?

title: Azure Key Vault
summary: Azure Key Vault stores secrets, certificates, and keys so pipelines and apps do not hardcode sensitive values.
content:
## Why it matters

Secrets should not live inside source code, pipeline YAML, or frontend bundles. Azure Key Vault stores sensitive values centrally and exposes them only to authorized pipelines, apps, or service identities.

## In CI/CD

A pipeline can read a secret at deploy time, inject it as an environment variable for the server, and keep it masked in logs.

## Frontend warning

Anything shipped to the browser is public. Use Key Vault for backend secrets, API credentials, signing keys, and deployment tokens, not for values that must be hidden inside client-side JavaScript.

related_questions.preview: How do you handle environment variables securely in Next.js?

title: Approvals, rollback, and monitoring
summary: Enterprise CI/CD adds controls around production releases: approvals, staged rollouts, rollback plans, logs, and monitoring.
content:
## Environment approvals

Production should not always deploy instantly after a build. Azure environments can require a manual approval from a lead, QA, or release manager before deployment continues.

## Rollback handling

A rollback usually means redeploying the previous artifact, swapping an App Service slot back, or moving a container deployment to the previous image tag.

## Monitoring

After deployment, teams watch application logs, error rates, performance metrics, and user-impact signals. The goal is to detect bad releases quickly and reverse them before customers are heavily affected.

related_questions.preview: How do you approach monitoring and alerting for a React application in production?
